mappings.rationaleAI: Broad coverage of European history 500-1500 CE maps onto High Middle Ages society (A.1), including social orders (A.1.a), agricultural growth (A.1.b), political institutions (A.1.g), England (A.5), and eastern European developments (A.7). The Black Death relates to late medieval plague effects (2.b).
Crusades driven by papal authority connect to the church in medieval society (A.1.d). Commercial effects relate to trade revival (A.1.b). Crusading in Iberia connects to the Reconquista (A.4).
The Renaissance aligns with the early Italian Renaissance (1.a), Humanism (1.a.i), new artistic concepts (1.a.ii), and Italian city-states (5.b, 5.c).
The Empire as protector of the papacy connects to A.1.g.i. Imperial governance relates to the Hohenstaufen dynasty (A.2.b), the Investiture Controversy (A.2.a), and later medieval Germany (4.a).

Directly covers England from the Norman Conquest to Edward I (A.5), including the Anglo-Norman monarchy (A.5.a), Plantagenets (A.5.b), Scotland/Wales/Ireland (A.5.c), and later medieval England (7.a).
The Norman Conquest directly addresses feudalism under William I (A.5.a), the Domesday survey (A.5.a.i), and central government strengthening (A.5.a.ii).
Magna Carta directly addresses King John's reign and baronial revolt (A.5.b.iii) and Henry III and the growth of Parliament (A.5.b.iv).
Institutional Christianity relates to the church in medieval society (A.1.d), Thomas Aquinas and Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii), and the Avignon papacy and Great Schism (3).
Machiavelli as Renaissance political theorist connects to the early Italian Renaissance (1.a) and Florentine republicanism (5.c.i).
Medieval British and Irish literature including The Canterbury Tales directly addresses Latin and vernacular literature (A.1.e.iii).
Chaucer's Canterbury Tales connects to vernacular literature (A.1.e.iii) and England under Edward III and Richard II (7.a.i).
Dante's works connect to medieval literature (A.1.e.iii) and Italian developments 1300-1400 (5.a).
Boethius, Abelard, and Ockham address Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii) and late medieval intellectual developments (1.c).
Aquinas's Summa Theologiae directly addresses Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii).
Viking activity connects to Scandinavian kingdoms (A.6) including expansion into Iceland and Greenland (A.6.b).
Aristocracy connects to the feudal nobility (A.1.a), kingship (A.1.g.ii), and declining feudal nobility (2.a).
Scottish independence connects to A.5.c.i. The Reformation relates to later Scottish developments (7.b).
Jewish practice connects to the status of Jews in medieval society (A.1.f).
Jewish persecution and diaspora directly address A.1.f.
The Black Death directly addresses plague effects (2.b).
Age of Discovery connects to Spanish explorations (8.c.ii) and Portuguese empire (8.d).
Habsburg consolidation connects to Rudolf of Habsburg (A.2.b.i) and later medieval Habsburgs (4.a).
Monastic types relate to church reform movements including Cistercians and mendicant orders (A.1.d).
Late medieval witchcraft beliefs connect to intellectual developments including witchcraft (1.c).
Spanish conquest connects to explorations in the early Age of Discovery (8.c.ii).
Reformation roots in late medieval church reform connect to the church in the later Middle Ages (3).
Dynastic rule connects to kingship (A.1.g.ii), the Hohenstaufen (A.2), Capetians (A.3), and Plantagenets (A.5).
